design_philosophy.core_loop: Collect resources (gold, wood, oil) → construct buildings and units → destroy opponent's base through military combat
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Economic efficiency vs. military production; territorial control vs. resource scarcity; tech progression unlocks vs. immediate defensive needs
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Command a fantasy army (Humans or Orcs) as a military strategist, managing both infrastructure and warfare on a dynamic battlefield with multiple unit types and tactical options
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Asymmetric faction design (Orcs strong in ground combat, Humans dominant in naval/air and spellcasting); non-renewable resources force strategic thinking about efficiency; terrain and forests are interactive obstacles, not just scenery; dual-objective campaigns mix pure destruction with rescue/escort missions, preventing single-strategy dominance

difficulty.continues_system: Campaign missions can be replayed; single-mission failure does not end game
difficulty.checkpointing: Mission-based progression; each campaign scenario is discrete
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Mission variety (some focus on resource management, others on rescue/escort objectives) creates natural difficulty variation; player can adjust opponent AI strength in custom scenarios via map editor

progression.upgrade_system: Tech tree unlocked via prerequisite buildings; worker efficiency upgradeable twice; advanced combat units unlock via building construction chains; spellcasters learned by advanced ground units
progression.unlock_mechanism: Building prerequisites gate access to advanced units and spellcasting; Human and Orc factions have parallel but asymmetric tech paths
replayability_hooks: Separate Human and Orc campaigns with different narratives and unit compositions
Map editor enables custom scenario creation for replayability against AI or multiplayer
Multiplayer support (up to 8 players) encourages competitive replay
Stand-alone scenarios offer varied strategic challenges beyond campaign

world_building_techniques: Faction-specific unit and building aesthetics (Human vs. Orc visual identity)
Campaign structure following 'The Second War' narrative arc
Mission briefings contextualizing objectives within larger conflict
Characters and story integrated into campaign progression

faction_indicators: Human faction: Medieval architecture, ranged superiority, spellcasting prowess, naval dominance
Orc faction: Tribal aesthetics, melee ground combat strength, invading force identity
Asymmetric unit rosters create distinct playstyle identities and faction loyalty
danger_signals: Enemy tech progression (advancement to advanced units indicates escalating threat)
Undefended resource nodes (vulnerable lumber/gold = strategic weakness)
Naval unit presence (indicates amphibious threat capability)
Spellcaster sightings (high-priority targets, indicate advanced opponent economy)
environmental_storytelling: Forests serve dual purpose as lumber source and tactical obstacle—forcing army routing decisions
Oil platforms indicate developed economy and tech advancement
Building damage/destruction visually conveys ongoing warfare
Fog of war creates exploration mystery and strategic information asymmetry
